chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk introduces the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), a fifteenth-century necromantic miscellany, and discusses its structure, content categories (illusionist, psychological, divinatory), and historical context. It includes a notable judicial record from 1437 involving Johannes Cunalis, a priest in Munich, who allegedly owned a book of necromancy; the demon Jubertus named—Luxuriosus, Superbus, Avarus—are explicitly listed. The manuscript contains rituals such as the Liber consecracionum and conjurations for spirits like Mirage, Lilith, and Semiforas.
key_passages: Interspersed with these are a version of the Liber consecracionum (no. 31); a list of spirits, with descriptions of the forms in which they appear and the functions they perform (no. 34);
The first two folios of the manuscript are missing, a circumstance which may help to explain how the manuscript evaded detection and thus survived.
One of the many subjects brought before the judges... was a sixty-year-old man, Jubertus of Bavaria... he served... Johannes Cunalis, who is a priest and plebanus, in a city called Munich...
...three demons, one named Luxuriosus, another Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils. And the first appeared to him in the form of a charming maiden of twelve years...
The mirror of Floron, for revelation of past, present and future

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk presents testimonial evidence from a trial involving Johannes Cunalis and his demon conjurations (Luxuriosus, Superbus, Avarus), describes ritual practices (cross-desecration, herb-gathering, poison-making), links these to the Munich Handbook CLM 849, and introduces Kieckhefer’s Table B classifying necromantic types. It also discusses manuscript compilation theory and possible authorship.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details two major illusion rituals from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849): an illusory banquet conjured by fifteen spirits using a sword-traced circle and hoopoe offering, and an illusory castle conjured by squire-spirits using milk-honey offerings. It includes spirit names, ritual instructions, circle diagrams, and ingredients like bat’s blood and seven herbs for flight ointments. The text also discusses the nature of demonic oaths and parallels with witchcraft narratives.
key_passages: You should have some book brought to them at once, and on it you should have them swear in this manner: ‘All twelve of us swear on this sacred book ... to come to you without delay whenever you invoke us, and to have tables laid, such as you have seen and even better.’

The circle to be used for this experiment is depicted: a quadruple band with a pentangle inscribed in the centre, a sword depicted at the top (extending downward across all four bands, with its point on the top of the pentangle) and other figures (likewise extending across all four bands) on the upper left, upper right, lower right, and lower left.

You must know that no matter how much they eat, they will be all the more hungry, because they will seem like dishes but they will not exist, so that if a famished person were to gorge himself, believing them to be real, he would no doubt die just as if he ate nothing.
The hoopoe is possessed of great virtue for necromancers and invokers of demons [nigromanticis et demones invocantibus], on which account we use it much for our safekeeping.
make an ointment from seven herbs, the fat of a goat and the blood of a bat, and smear it on one’s face, hands and chest

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details several necromantic experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on obtaining illusionary transportation (horse, ship, flying throne) and invisibility. It includes spirit names (e.g., Lautrayth, Feremin, Oliroomim; Firiel, Melemil, Berith, Taraor), ritual circles with inscribed names and diagrams, ingredients (bridle, horsehide, bat’s blood, black cat, heliotrope seed, bird fat/blood, etc.), and incantations. It also references cross-textual sources like Socrates’ book of magic and the Rawlinson manuscript.
key_passages: O Lautrayth, Feremin, [and] Oliroomim, spirits who attend upon sinners, 1, so- such-like, trusting in your power, conjure you by Him who spoke and [all things] were made...

The figure shown is a single circular band, with a square inscribed, and a circle inside the square. Short bars extend outward from the middle of each side of the square and touch the inner side of the circular band.
I conjure you, O Fyriel, Mememil, Berith, [and] Taraor, powerful, magnificent, illustrious spirits, in whom I place all my trust, by the one, inseparable and undivided Trinity...
...writes a series of names on the door of a vacant house, at dusk, with bat’s blood, then withdraws for a brief time. When he returns he finds a horse awaiting him.
When you wish to infuse a spirit into a dead person... around the outside these names should be carved: Brimer, Suburith, Tranauit; on the inside, these names: Lyroth, Beryen, Damayn.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details a complex resuscitation and illusionist necromantic ritual from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including spirit conjurations (Brimer, Suburith, six spirits, horseman demon), use of a consecrated ring to simulate death/resurrection, and associated ingredients (hoopoe blood/feather for circle drawing, white cloth for storage). It includes detailed descriptions of the ritual steps across multiple days, the structure of the magical ring with nested bands of spirit names, and theological commentary on how these practices parody Christ’s Resurrection and align with medieval views of Antichristian illusion.
key_passages: When you have said this once, six spirits will at once appear at the circle, requesting the ring, which you will give them. When you have given it, they will depart...
You will take the ring, saying to him, 'Thanks to you and to them.' When you have said this, he will at once withdraw; and you too shall depart from the circle, destroying it completely.
Keep this ring with you, wrapped in a sheet of white cloth. When you wish to cause someone to appear dead... place this ring on his finger, and he will appear to be a corpse...
The figure shown for this experiment is a double circular band with a pentagram inscribed. The names carved on the ring are repeated, along with astrological signs, within the two bands.
The reference to the monumentum , the white shroud, and Sunday morning before dawn all make it clear that this resuscitation is intended as a replication or perhaps a parody of Christ’s Resurrection.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk analyzes psychological experiments in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on harmful and favour-granting magic. It details three key rituals: (1) sowing enmity using carved stones buried under thresholds, burned, cooled, and beaten; (2) inflicting dementia via conjuration of Mirael and candle magic; and (3) gaining favour through image carving, metal casting, and binding with spirits like Dyacom. The text emphasizes sympathetic magic principles and demonstrates how demonic invocations are secondary to imitative acts.
key_passages: On one you must carve the name of one person, with the names Cartutay, Momabel, Sobil and Geteritacon. On the other [you must carve] the name of the other person and the names Puzanil, Pimaton, Folfitoy, and Mansator. These names are highly antipathetic to each other.
I conjure all you hateful and malignant, invidious and discordant demons ... to arouse at once between so-and-so and so-and-so as much hatred as there was between Cain and Abel.

On his forehead carve the name ‘Dyacom’; on his chest, the three names ‘Pumeon’, ‘Terminas’, and ‘Peripaos’; and on [his] shoulders the six names ‘Madam’, ‘Fabni’, ‘Gebel’, ‘Darail’, ‘Vmeloth’ and ‘Thereoth’; and on his stomach the name ‘Byreoth’.
As I have separated these stones, thus may so-and-so separate himself from so-and-so, and may they be as distant as these stones.
Just as this candle is extinguished, so may all the power in N. be utterly consumed.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details a complex love magic ritual from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), involving inscribing demon names on an image of the target woman made from parchment of a female dog in heat, using dove blood and eagle quill. It includes fumigation with myrrh and saffron, suspension of the image, and a final summoning circle containing eleven spirit names (e.g., Rator, Lampoy) to compel the woman’s presence via six angelic-appearing spirits. The ritual is framed as non-dangerous and highly effective, even attributed to Solomon.
key_passages: ‘I draw so-and-so, N., daughter of so-and-so, whom I wish to have, in the name of these six hot spirits, namely Tubal, Satan, Reuces, Cupido, Afalion, Duliatus, that she may love me above all others living in this world.’
‘As you, Satan, and you, Reuces, are inscribed on this image made in the name of so-and-so, may you so afflict her arms without delay, so that she can do nothing but desire to embrace me.’
I conjure all you demons inscribed on this image, by your lords to whom you are bound in obedience — Sobedon, Badalam and Berith — that you should inflame so-and-so…

The circle is shown as a single band containing the names Rator, Lampoy, Despan, Brunlo, Dronoth, Maloqui, Satola, Gelbid, Mascifm, Nartim and Lodoni. The centre of the circle is labelled ‘Place of the Master’.

When she enters the circle and you greet her, remember to touch her with the image that you have around your neck, and on this account she will love you for all eternity and will not care to see anyone but you.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details two main erotic magic experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849): Experiment 12 uses wax image-making with angelic/demonic invocations (Belial, Astaroth, Paymon), timing under planetary hours (Venus/Jove/Mars/Sun/Saturn), and piercing with needles to compel love; Experiment 13 involves inscribing spirit names on animal shoulderblades or ribs and heating them in fire while conjuring infernal spirits (Asyel, Castiel, Bel, Ebal) to constrain or incite a person’s will through symbolic burning. The text also references parallels in ancient Greek magic, Picatrix, and other medieval sources.
key_passages: "Thou, Belial, and thou Astaroth, and thou, Paymon, be my helpers in this undertaking."

"Just as this needle is fixed in the heart of this image, so may the love of N. be fixed to the love of N., so that she cannot sleep, wake, lie down, sleep, [or] walk until she burns with love of me."
"Asyel, Castiel, Lamisniel, Rabam, Erlain, [and] Belam, I command you..."

"writing the names of the ‘infernal spirits’ Bel and Ebal on a rib, and in between his own name and that of the woman he wishes to seduce. Then he burns the rib, and when it is hot he should conjure Bel and Ebal not to rest until they cause the woman’s heart to burn..."
"the experiment was taken [from the book] On the Secret Arts of the Imaginary Art, [from the book] On the Flowers of All Experiments"

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk focuses on divinatory rituals in the Munich Handbook (Clm 849), especially scrying with mirrors, crystals, and fingernails. It details spirits like Floron and Lilith, ritual participants (especially virgin boys), and materials used (steel, balsam, aloes, olive oil). It also contextualizes these practices within broader medieval concerns about false accusation and demonic deception.
key_passages: Have a mirror made of pure steel... have it bright and shiny like a sword. And have it made in the name of Floron, and around the rim of the mirror... inscribe these ten names...
She will appear in a mirror with two or more servants, and will tell the truth or give appropriate signs regarding a theft, murder, or other circumstance.
scrape well the thumb nail of his left hand with a knife. Then bind to the same thumb, beneath the nail, a slip with the following names written on it...
I command you to be bound beneath the deep waters of the sea, by these two names, Joth [and] Nabnoth, by which Solomon bound demons in a glass vessel.
The master himself should be bathed and dressed in clean garments. Before he begins to exercise this work he should sprinkle in the air honey, milk and wine, mixed together in equal proportions...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details a ritual from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849) for acquiring knowledge through demonic conjuration, focusing on the invocation of three demon kings—Apolin, Maraloth, and Berith—to send a teacher-spirit. It outlines the use of bird blood and heart to inscribe names on cloth, the construction of a ritual circle, sleep-based spirit communication, and symbolic exchange of a sword representing authority over knowledge. Kieckhefer compares this practice with Jewish angelic magic, the ars notoria, and Caesarius of Heisterbach’s story of a schoolboy who made homage to the devil.
key_passages: Apolin, Maraloth, Berith, 1, so-
...the markings on this cloth are to be made with the blood of a bird, perhaps a hoopoe, and evidently the magician is to use the heart of this bird as a writing instrument...
The circle required for this first experiment is a single band inscribed within a square. Inscribed in and around the band are the names of seven spirits and the four cardinal directions.
...you must have a tablet, and must write down all that he tells you.
O Apolin, Maraloch, Berith, Sathan, Beliath, Belzebuc, [and] Lucifer, 1 implore you to command the master - here naming his name - to come to me tomorrow before sunrise...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk focuses on the structure and function of conjurations in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), analyzing their formal elements: declaration, address, invocations, and instruction. It includes detailed examples from nos. 3 and 7, listing named spirits (e.g., Rator et al.; Vsyr et al.) and divine/demonic names used for coercion. A narrative exemplum involving Master Peter of Ardene and a Spanish magician illustrates ritual summoning and demon responses tied to liturgical time. The text also notes linguistic features (Latin vs. vernacular), cross-cultural comparisons with Jewish magic, and references to other manuscripts like the Rawlinson and pseudo-Chaldean texts.
key_passages: The conjurations are so centrally important to necromantic experiments that the art of necromancy...can even be referred to simply as the conjuring of spirits.
[Declaration:] I conjure [Address:] you demons inscribed in this circle, to whom is given the power of seducing and binding women in the love of men...

O Vsyr, Salaul, Silitor, Demor, Zanno, Syrtroy, Risbel, Cutroy, Lytay, Onor, Moloy, Pumotor, Tami, Oor and Ym, arms-bearing spirits, whose role it is to bear arms and to deceive human senses wherever you wish...
by these names, which cause you fear and terror: Rator, Lampoy, Despan, Brulo, Dronoth, Maloqui, Satola, Gelbid, Mascifm, Nartim and Lodoni...
the terms ‘conjuration’ and ‘exorcism’ are essentially interchangeable in medieval usage

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the structure and content of conjurations in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), especially focusing on invocations used to compel spirits. It catalogues nine categories of invocation—divine persons, sacred names, events in Christ’s life, saints, Virgin Mary, angels, material creation, Last Judgement, and demonic rulers—and lists numerous specific examples. Several named demons are mentioned (e.g., Lylet, Oriens, Toth, Astaroth), along with their ranks and subordinate spirits. The text also discusses how sacred names (Tetragrammaton, Adonay, El, etc.) function as magical keys to control spirits.
key_passages: one conjuration addresses the demon Lylet along with her companions (socii), who remain unnamed.
by Toth, your prince; by Zambrim et Mambrim; by Vsuel [and] by Saduel, to whom you are held to obey and tell the truth
by your master Astaroth
When Oriens is conjured, the invocations refer to his own dignities and subordinate spirits: 'by your kings, and by all your royal powers; by Sotuem [and] Sortfen...'
one invokes the demonic names Apolyn, Gebel, Astaroth, Tereol, Falmar and Tyroces

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk analyzes the structure and content of necromantic conjurations in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), especially focusing on the conjuration of Mirage and Satan. It compares these to mainstream exorcism traditions, highlighting formal parallels between official exorcisms and forbidden necromantic rituals. The text shows how both genres use divine authority, sacred names, and threats to compel spirits.
key_passages: The conjuration begins anomalously with a statement regarding the powers being brought to bear against the Devil (‘may the angels and archangels accuse you ... may the elect of God accuse you’), followed by a prayer for divine aid...

[Come] to me in this hour, without any harm or injury or affliction to my soul or my body, or send another [spirit] or have him come, who will know perfectly how to fulfil my every desire, and will not withdraw from me until I have given him leave and he has fully satisfied my will.

The Word made flesh commands you, He who was born of the virgin commands you, Jesus of Nazareth commands you, He who created you commands you to carry out quickly what I ask or wish to have from you or desire to know...
It is not I who command you, nor my sins, O most unclean spirit, but the immaculate lamb, Jesus Christ Our Lord, the Son of God, commands you.
The angels press upon [urguent] you, the archangels press upon you, the prophets press upon you, the apostles press upon you, the martyrs press upon you, the confessors press upon you.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the spirit hierarchy and functions in CLM 849 (Munich Handbook), including ranks (marquis, president, count, duke, prince, king), appearances (human, beastly, hybrid), and functions (treasure revelation, knowledge delivery, love magic, transportation). It discusses theological debates about whether spirits are fallen, unfallen, or intermediate; the use of rituals like fumigation, fasting, oaths, and sword circles to compel obedience; and lists of named spirits with their attributes. The text emphasizes that magicians see themselves as commanding demons, not serving them.
key_passages: ‘And you should note carefully that in this ship you are able to mention holy things, as in true Christianity, because these spirits are between good and evil, dwelling neither in Hell nor in Paradise.’
There is no explicit acknowledgement in the handbook that the magician is serving the spirits; to the contrary, he sees himself as commanding and exploiting them.
The spirits are conjured to appear in human form, because they are assumed closer to beasts, and thus it is more natural for them to assume grotesque versions of bestial appearance
Each has command of legions of subordinate spirits (perhaps an allusion to Mark 5:9), as few as twenty-two, and as many as fifty.
The writer makes no pretence that his magic is in any way ‘natural’, as opposed to demonic. [...] the commanding of malign spirits is the essence of his art.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk provides detailed analysis of magic circles in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including their structure, inscriptions (spirit names, sacred phrases), and layout. It lists numerous named spirits—especially demons like Belial, Astaroth, Satan, Lucifer, Berith—and describes how they appear in circle diagrams with positional instructions for magicians and mediums. Sacred names (Agla, Tetragrammaton, Alpha et O) are used alongside spirit names to enhance efficacy. Ritual tools include circles inscribed on ground or vellum, pentagrams, swords, candles, and censers; ingredients include verbena and cemetery stones.
key_passages: The circle for no. 12 gives a modified conjuration [...] Tu Belial, tu Astaroth, tu Paymon, ad hoc sitis opus michi adiutores.
No. 1 has the names of the spirits Belial, Satan, Osendior, Matalot, Belzebub, Lucifer, Sententino and Apolin inscribed in and around its band.

The position of the master is often marked in the centre of the circle, with the term Locus magistri [...] No. 22 [...] Hie sederat puer super signum (at the top), and Hie sedeat magister super signum a latere sinistro (at the bottom).
Inscriptions are of various kinds: 1. Four cardinal directions, with east on top [...]; 2. Positions of the master and child medium [...]; 3. Names of spirits, given most frequently within the bands.
No. 40 has [...] ft* Hunc circulum facio in nomine Patris omnipotentis dei, qui solo verbo cuncta creauit.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains three rituals from the Munich Handbook: (1) No. 4 for gaining dignity/honour, involving spirit conjurations with named spirits like Dyacom and companions, image-making in stone/lead/silver, and binding rituals; (2) No. 5 for arousing hatred between friends, using inscribed river stones with demonic names like Cartutay and Puzanil groups, fire, and exorcisms invoking demons such as Apolyon and Astaroth; (3) No. 6 & 7 for banquet and castle summonings, respectively, involving circles with 15–16 spirit names (e.g., Oymelor group, Vsyr group), ritual tools like sword and turtle dove, and diagrams. Includes detailed instructions on materials, circle/diagram construction, incantations in Latin, and cross-references to other rituals.
key_passages: O Dyacon, Pumeon, Termines, Peripaos, Midain, Fabin, Gebel, Dorayl, Vmeloth, Tereoth, et Bireoth, spiritus benignissimi et concordiam seminantes...
Coniuro vos spiritus inimicissimos, per eterni dei gloriam, quod inter talem et talem...quantum odium inter vos est, tantum inter ipsos seminetis et inseratis.
Oymelor, Demefin, Lamair, Masair, Symofor, Rodobayl, Tentetos, Lotobor, Memoyr, Tamafin, Leutaber, Tatomofon, Faubair, Selutabel, Rimasor, Syrama, spiritus iocundissimi...
O Vsyr, Salaul, Silitor, Demor, Zanno, Syrtroy, Risbel, Cutroy, Lytay, Onor, Moloy, Pumotor, Tami, Oor, et Ym, spiritus armigeri...
Hoc enim experimentum 2 vsus fuit Parmen[i]des ad regis Persarum graciam obtinendam.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains detailed ritual descriptions from the Munich Handbook of necromancy (CLM 849), as analyzed by Kieckhefer. It includes: (1) a military illusion ritual invoking spirits like Salaul and the 'armigeri' group to create phantom armies and fortresses; (2) a boat summoning ritual requiring a dead person's rib, fumigation with marrow, and invocation of eight water-dwelling spirits (Fyrin et al.); (3) a horse conjuration invoking three spirits (Lautrayth, Feremin/Oliroomim) using an unused bridle; and (4) resurrection magic involving a gold ring inscribed with demon names (Brimer, Suburith, etc.) and consecrated over five days in running water. Each ritual includes precise timing (moon phases, weekdays), directional requirements, circle diagrams, Latin incantations, and instructions for ritual construction/destruction.
key_passages: O Vsyr, Salaul, Silitol, Denior, Zaimo, Syrtroy, Ristel, Cutroy, Lytoy, Onor, Moloy, Pumiotor, Tamy, Dor, Ym, voco vos vt hie venire debeatis per consecracionem huius circuli...
Ego sum vnus de vocatis spiritibus, ab aliis ad te missus, qui nominor Salaul, cui precipias quid tibi placet petere, et statim fiet.
Fyrin (quod- sic dicas: O Fyrin), Dyspil, Onoroy, Sysabel, Cotroy, Tyroy, Orooth, Rimel, spiritus- habitatores aque, qui naues nauigantium periclitare conamini, ego vos coniuro et exorcizo...
O Lautrayth, Feremin, [et] Oliroomim, spiritus peccatoribus insistentes, ego, talis, in vestra virtute confidens, vos coniuro per ilium qui dixit et facta sunt...

Coniuro vos, omnes demones scriptos in hoc anulo - quem in manibus habeas, per Patrem et Filium et Spiritum Sanctum... vt in eo hec virtus existat, uidelicet quod quandocumque inposuero ipsum in digito alicuius mortui, vna uobis ipsum ingrediatur...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details several magical operations: an invisibility ritual using spirits and incense, a love ritual involving wax images and needles, a binding ritual using heated bone spatulas, and the summoning of a flying throne via elemental jars. It also includes methods for finding things in dreams and obtaining horses through specific names and bat blood.
key_passages: Tracto eciam de arte invisibilitatis, hodie quasi ab omnibus ignorata.
Tu Belial, tu Astarothtf tu Paymon, ad hoc sitis opus michi adiutores.
Per hoc experimentum constringitur homo, mulier, vir, spiritus, [vel] bestia, cuiuscumque condicionis existat.
voca regem nubium, nomine Bartha, vt mittat 3es de ducibus qui habent posse [super] nubibus
Ad inveniendum in sompnis que vis, scribe hec nomina in carta virginea

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details several magical mirror and crystal rituals from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including two versions of the Speculum Floron for revelation, a third method using mirrors in Jupiter-Venus conjunction, and the First Mirror of Lilith (Lylet) for divination. It also includes rituals for invisibility (using a black cat born in March), theft/murder detection via crystal or boy’s gaze, and Pluto’s Key to open locks. Ritual ingredients include calibe mirrors, balsam, aloes, amber, myrrh, white frankincense, human blood, cat organs, elyotropia seeds, and mixed mel/lac/vinum. Incantations are largely in pseudo-Chaldaic or Latin with divine names (Alpha et O, Sabaoth, Emanuel). Timing follows planetary days (Venus, Friday) and lunar phases (waxing moon).
key_passages: O Floron, in speculo sicut solitus es apparere citissime respondeas.
Coniuro vos demones vt veniatis et compareatis et dicatis michi de quocumque interrogauero vos omnem veritatem, in nomine Patris et Filij et Spiritus Sancti. Amen.
Studeas invenire vnam cattam nigram, natam in mense Martij, et exviscera earn... Sapreson lampsones sampsanay, invisibilis fiat homo.
Coniuro te, puer, • per Patrem et Filium et Spiritum Sanctum... vt quidquid tu videos nobis insinues...
Sume tibi ferrum inventum ex inprouisu, et fac tibi fieri clauem huiusmodi, factam die Veneris.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains detailed ritual instructions for spirit conjuration using child seers (puer/virgo), including scrying on ram’s shoulder blade and basin, binding spirits by divine names (Tetragramaton, Alpha et O, Elthie), and invoking angelic hierarchies. It lists numerous spirit names (e.g., Discobermath, Bethala, Carab) and ritual ingredients (resin, olive oil, incense). Also includes the prologue to the Liber consecracionum, outlining purification requirements for practitioners.
key_passages: Coniuro te, Sona, per hoc nomen domini: Tetragramaton, et per hoc nomen dei: loth, et per hoc nomen dei: Nabnoth...
Vos demones ab oriente,- vos demones ab occidente, vos demones ab meridie, vos demones a septentrione, aperite nobis, Discobermath, Archidemath, Fritath...
accipe spatulam arietis dextram, oleo oliue invnctam, quam ponendo super manubrium cultelli, inspiciat puer- virgo.
Hec nomina dices in aurem pueri virginis: Carab, Riasteli, Careb, Basal, Mistal, Oronothel, Fabanin, Non, Va, Vatuel.
ab omni pollucione mentis et corporis se debet abstinere in cibo [et] potu...nouem diebus ante opus inceptum...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains extensive Latin consecration prayers for the Munich Handbook of necromancy (CLM 849), focusing on divine invocations to empower ritual texts and spirit-binding experiments. It lists numerous sacred names (e.g., On, Alpha et O, Tetragramaton, Emanuel) and emphasizes their power over elemental forces (earth, air, fire, water) and spirits (both celestial and infernal). Ritual instructions include nine-day book consecration, use of holy water, the sign of the cross, Confiteor recitation, and psalms. Biblical figures like Moses, Noah, Daniel, and Jonah are invoked for their exorcistic authority.
key_passages: Hoc factum per ix dies, erit liber consecratus, et quocienscumque aliud consecrare volueris, incipias primam oracionem legere, scilicet Deus invisibilis...
...per sanctissima nomina tua - On, Alpha et O, El, Eloye, On, Otheon, Stimlamathon,-Ezelphares, Tetragramaton...
Quo audito a mare regreditur, aer conmutatur,- vel N. concitatur,- terra tremit, ignis extingwitur, omnisque exercitus terrenus et infernalis tremet...
...per sanctissimum nomen tuum quod scribitur 4or litteris, scilicet Ioth, Varo, Hee, Teth, quo audito mare regreditur...
Hoc factum per ix dies, erit liber consecratus...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk presents the conjuration of the demon Mirage (also called Satan), drawn from folios 59v–62v and parallel passages in CLM 849. It includes extensive invocations of divine names, biblical figures (patriarchs, prophets, apostles), and sacramental elements (cross, Eucharist, baptismal authority) to compel obedience. The ritual combines Christian liturgical language with magical formulae, invoking divine power over demons through Christ’s Passion, the Trinity, and sacred names like Tetragrammaton and Emanuel.
key_passages: Adiuro te, Mirage, non mea vi set uirtute Spiritus Sancti, ut sis subiectus mandatis meis, ea sine mora perficienda. Cede ministris Ihesu Christi.

Exorcizo te, maledicte et mendacissime spiritus, per verbum veritatis, per omnipotentem, per Ihesum Christum Nazarenum, agnum immaculatum de altissimo progression, de Spiritu Sancto conceptual, ex Maria virgine natum.
Imperat tibi dominus. Imperat tibi magestas. Imperat tibi deus Pater. Imperat tibi Filius. Imperat tibi Spiritus Sanctus.
Coniuro et constringo atque precipio tibi, vt sine aliquo nocumento michi nunc et semper obedias, sine lesione et grauamine corporis et anime mee.
Per omnia quibus constringi et ligari possis... per sigillum Salominis, et per anulum - eius, ix candarias celestes...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains detailed excerpts from the Munich Handbook of necromancy (CLM 849), as analyzed by Kieckhefer. It includes spirit conjurations for obtaining hidden treasures and knowledge, a mirror-based divination ritual involving cemetery stones and verbena, a list of named demons with ranks and legions, a love spell using inscribed bone burned in fire, and instructions for drawing protective circles with divine names (Agla, Sabaoth, Tetragramaton, Adonay) while reciting psalms and making the sign of the cross.
key_passages: Coniuro te, Astra, Astaroth, Cebal, per Patrem et Filium et Spiritual Sanctum... vt obediatis michif

Barbaras [est] magnus comes et dux... Habet sub se xxxvj legiones.
Coniuro vos spiritus infernales Bel et Ebal, quorum nomina in ista costa scripta sunt et in igne ardescunt, per omnes virtutes celorum et terre...
Hunc circulum facio in nomine dei Patris omnipotentis... Hunc circulum facio in nomine dei viui... Hunc circulum facio in nomine Spiritus Sancti paracliti.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains several magical experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including: a spirit conjuration circle with triple bands and Trinitarian inscriptions; a dream-based treasure-divination ritual invoking 'Oriens' and requiring penitential prayers, repeated invocations, and almsgiving; the great name 'Semiforas' used in magical operations; a horse-summoning rite involving Hebrew names written in bat blood and a binding incantation; protective spirit names for averting harm (Layalym et al.); an invisibility ritual using a white dove sacrificed on Saturday night and drawing with its blood; and a chemical recipe involving lunar salts, vitriol, mercury, wax, and various herbs/resins. Page references span 105v–108v.
key_passages: Hunc circulum facio in nomine Patris omnipotentis dei, qui solo verbo cuncta creauit...
per Sotuem, Sortfen... vt in nocte sequenti veniat ad me in sompnis Haram, spiritus benignus...

Nunc igitur sume albam columbam, die Sabbati de nocte, et cartam agni virgineam...
Item recipe salis petri, vitrioli, rorani,- ana tere, et fac aquam fortem ex eis...

chunk_summaries.summary: This final chunk of Kieckhefer’s *Forbidden Rites* study includes index entries and brief references to ritual elements in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), especially focusing on named spirits (demons and angels), ritual implements (circles, swords, mirrors), ingredients (candles, hoopoes), and textual sources. It also lists specific folios with diagrams: seals for planets/days of the week (fols. 73r–74v), Seal of Solomon (fol. 95v), tripartite prayer circles (fols. 99v, 103r), Trinitarian concentric bands (fol. 105v), and a complex character (fol. 108r).
key_passages: Fol. 73r, no. 37c: Seals for planets and days of the week.
Fol. 95v, no. 37r: Seal of Solomon (three characters).
Fol. 105v, no. 40: Three concentric bands; centre of innermost divided into inscribed quadrants; each circle dedicated to member of Trinity.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk introduces the conceptual framing of magical books as sacred objects requiring consecration, focusing on the Liber consecrationum and its elaborate re-consecration rituals. It details divine names invoked in these rites (e.g., Tetragramaton, Sabaoth), ritual components (holy water, stole, litany), and distinguishes necromantic conjuring from exorcism only by intent. The Munich Handbook is identified as a central clerical source of demonic magic to be analyzed later.
key_passages: A short, anonymous work called the Liber consecrationum (Book of Consecrations)... makes clear that the book itself was a sacred object requiring elaborate consecration...

by the power of these your most sacred names, On, Jesus Christ, Alpha and O, El, Eloy, Eloyye, Sithothith, Eon, Sepmelamaton, Ezelphates, Tetragramaton, Elyoram, Ryon, Deseryon, Erystion, Ysyornus, Onela, Baysyn, Moyn, Messias, Sother, Emanuel, Sabaoth, Adonay...

What we now call exorcism was practised by an individual... What we now call conjuring was also practised by an individual... In neither case was the command automatically efficacious: both exorcists and conjurers reckoned on the possibility that the demons might resist their invocations...
the Munich handbook of necromancy... is a characteristically clerical form of magic, using Latin texts and presupposing knowledge of mainstream ritual.

The rites of magic suggest questions analogous to those raised by any rites... it is better to perceive demonic magic as an illicit form of religion than as a cultural phenomenon distinct from religion.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk introduces the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), a mid-15th-century German necromantic miscellany containing 42+ experiments categorized as illusionist, psychological, and divinatory. It details the structure of the manuscript, including named rituals like the Liber consecracionum, conjurations involving demons such as Mirage and Lilith, tools like the mirror of Floron, and spirit names (Semiforas, Beheydraz, Anleyuz, Manitaynus). The text emphasizes practical ritual use over theoretical coherence.
key_passages: The compiler of this main block of material was evidently German... a miscellany, with little linkage, clustering or other organization of its materials, and no effort to develop a coherent theory

All but a few of the experiments fall into the three main categories. There are twelve illusionist experiments... Seven psychological experiments... Fully seventeen experiments are divinatory techniques...
The first two folios of the manuscript are missing, a circumstance which may help to explain how the manuscript evaded detection and thus survived.
A list of spirits, with descriptions of the forms in which they appear and the functions they perform (no. 34)
Braekman’s no. 20... calls upon ‘the power and might of these spirits, Beheydraz, Anleyuz, [and] Manitaynus’, to aid in the magical seduction of a woman.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk situates the Munich Handbook (CLM 849) in its historical context, focusing on late medieval necromancy in and around Munich. It discusses the trial of Jubertus of Bavaria, who confessed to conjuring three demons—Luxuriosus, Superbus, and Avarus—from a book owned by the priest Johannes Cunalis. The chapter also examines Johannes Hartlieb’s classification of necromancy as the first forbidden art and his description of demonic pacts and rituals. It explores possible links between Hartlieb, CLM 849, and clerical magical practice, while analyzing the manuscript’s compilation process and scribal characteristics.
key_passages: ...when he who spoke opened this book at once there appeared to him three demons, one named Luxuriosus, another Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils.
Hartlieb lists necromancy (nigramancia) as the first of the seven forbidden arts, along with geomancy, hydromancy, aeromancy, pyromancy, chiromancy and spatulamancy.
...one who wishes to exercise this art must give all sorts of sacrifices to the devils, and must make an oath and pact [verpintnuß] with the devils.
The writer’s own orthography was highly erratic; within the same experiment he sometimes slipped from one version to another in his names for demons...
What seems quite clear is that the writer was a cleric – probably a priest, and at least a person in minor orders.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk discusses the Munich Handbook (Clm 849), focusing on its 'illusionist' necromantic experiments—those producing illusory spectacles like flying horses, banquets, castles, and invisibility. It highlights connections to other traditions (e.g., Arabic astral magic, Jewish divination, and later witch-trial concepts) and cites named spirits such as Mirage (with Satan as alternative), along with ritual tools like the Tetragrammaton ring and ingredients like hoopoe/bat blood. The Rawlinson manuscript’s flying horse rite is analyzed in detail and linked to the Thesaurus spirituum/De nigromancia tradition.
key_passages: a conjuration edited from two versions in the later folios is addressed to a spirit named Mirage, while an alternative form in the main block addresses Satan.
The ceremony given here for conjuring the horse is borrowed, perhaps directly, from the De nigromancia or Thesaurus spirituum ascribed to Roger Bacon.
Twelve of the experiments in Clm 849 are ‘illusionist’ ones, intended to make things appear other than as they are...
Socrates himself, he claims, speaks of the power of one circle (no. 9) in his book of magic.
the banquet that demons bring forth is in one particular like the feasts ascribed in later years to witches at their sabbaths: the food does not really exist...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details two major illusionist experiments from the Munich handbook: the conjuration of a lavish banquet (no. 6) and an illusory castle (no. 7). It describes specific rituals involving timing, tools like swords and hoopoes, and ingredients such as milk, honey, and magical ointments.
key_passages: First one must invoke fifteen spirits... carrying a shining sword and a hoopoe
to travel quickly wherever one wishes, one should make an ointment from seven herbs, the fat of a goat and the blood of a bat
The following experiment (no. 7) is designed to obtain an illusory castle, with defenders.
taking milk and honey with you, some of a which you must sprinkle in the air.
the hoopoe is possessed of great virtue for necromancers and invokers of demons

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details several ritual experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including methods for obtaining illusory transportation (horses, ships, flying throne), invisibility (via cloak, plant, or sacrifice), and manipulation of life/death states using inscribed rings. It provides detailed conjurations, spirit names (e.g., Lautrayth, Feremin, Oliroomim; Firiel, Melemil, Berith, Taraor; Brimer, Suburith, Tranauit, Lyroth, Beryen, Damayn), protective circles, ritual ingredients (bridle, bat’s blood, horsehide, heliotrope seeds), and biblical references used in incantations. The text also notes connections to other traditions like the Rawlinson manuscript and classical motifs such as the ring of Gyges.
key_passages: O Lautrayth, Feremin, [and] Oliroomim, spirits who attend upon sinners, I, so-and-so, trusting in your power, conjure you by Him who spoke and [all things] were made...
When you have said this three times, you will see three knights come from afar... they will at once dismount from their horses and greet you cheerfully.
I conjure you, O Fyriel, Mememil, Berith, [and] Taraor, powerful, magnificent, illustrious spirits, in whom I place all my trust, by the one, inseparable and undivided Trinity...

When you wish to infuse a spirit into a dead person... have a ring made of gold. Around the outside these names should be carved: Brimer, Suburith, Tranauit; on the inside, these names: Lyroth, Beryen, Damayn.
...if the circle given above is inscribed on horsehide with the blood of a horse and with the tooth of a white horse, and then is shown to horses, they will die at once.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details necromantic experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on a spirit-conjuration rite involving a horseman who delivers a consecrated ring used to control life/death appearances. It includes descriptions of protective circles, ritual ingredients (hoopoe blood and feather), named spirits inscribed on magical rings, and theological reflections on Antichrist’s deceptive miracles. The text also references parallel traditions in Rawlinson MS D 252 and compares medieval illusionist magic to theological debates about demonic deception.
key_passages: you will see toward the east someone coming on horseback, who, when he arrives at the circle, will say, ‘So-and-so’ – naming the names written above – ‘send you this consecrated ring…'

When you wish a corpse to appear animated, place the ring as aforesaid… and within an hour it will arise in the form to which it is previously accustomed, and will speak before all with a living voice, and will be able to display this quality for six days
If you draw it on a Friday with the feather of a hoopoe and with its blood on a freshly prepared sheet of parchment…
The figure shown for this experiment is a double circular band with a pentagram inscribed. The names carved on the ring are repeated, along with astrological signs, within the two bands.
pretending to resurrect the dead was one specialty associated traditionally with Antichrist.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details psychological experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on harm-inducing rituals (nos. 2 and 5) that use sympathetic magic—e.g., stones, images, burial—and invoke demonic spirits like Mirael or antipathetic names (Cartutay–Geteritacon; Puzanil–Mansator). It contrasts these with a more elaborate favor-gaining ritual (no. 4), which inscribes eleven demonic names on tin images and binds them symbolically. The text also references parallel traditions in the Liber de angelis (e.g., Vindicta Troie) and Picatrix, highlighting the Munich Handbook’s preference for imitative over natural-ingredient magic.
key_passages: On one you must carve the name of one person, with the names Cartutay, Momabel, Sobil and Geteritacon. On the other [you must carve] the name of the other person and the names Puzanil, Pimaton, Folfitoy, and Mansator. These names are highly antipathetic to each other.

I conjure you most inimical spirits, by the glory of the everlasting God, to sow and arouse as much hatred between so-and-so and so-and-so, whose names are carved here on these stones, as there is between you.

O Dyacon, Pumeon, Termines, Peripaos, Midain, Fabin, Gebel, Dorayl, Vmeloth, Tereoth and Bireoth, most benevolent spirits who sow concord [...] that by this image made in the name of so-and-so you may bind him to me in such a manner that he will revere me above all mortals.

The psychological experiments in the Munich handbook are generally much longer and more fully developed than the materials in these Arabic sources. What they add are chiefly conjurations and operations of sympathetic magic.
Even of all the experiments in the Munich manual, only two (nos 2 and 5), are designed specifically and exclusively to cause harm, and the damage is specifically psychological

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details necromantic love magic from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on a complex ritual involving inscribed parchment images, demon conjurations, and protective circles. It includes named spirits (e.g., Tubal, Satan, Astaroth, Zagam) used in sympathetic and coercive operations to compel love or bring victims to the magician. The text also references judicial cases and parallels with other grimoires like the Liber de angelis.
key_passages: ‘I draw so-and-so, N., daughter of so-and-so, whom I wish to have, in the name of these six hot spirits, namely Tubal, Satan, Reuces, Cupido, Afalion, Duliatus, that she may love me above all others living in this world.’
O Zagam, accept this dove which I sacrifice to you! ... I ask you, O Zagam, and all your companions, to bring forth [the desired woman]...

The circle is shown as a single band containing the names Rator, Lampoy, Despan, Brunlo, Dronoth, Maloqui, Satola, Gelbid, Mascifin, Nartim and Lodoni. The centre of the circle is labelled ‘Place of the Master’.
I conjure you demons inscribed in this circle... by these names which cause you terror, namely Rator, Lampoy, [etc.]...
When she enters the circle and you greet her, remember to touch her with the image that you have around your neck, and on this account she will love you for all eternity...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details erotic image magic and bone-based constraint rituals from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on necromantic experiments involving wax images pierced with needles, invocation of spirits like Belial, Astaroth, Paymon, Asyel, Castiel, Lamisniel, Rabam, Erlain, Olam, and Belam, as well as infernal pairings such as Bel and Ebal. It describes ritual ingredients (virgin wax, white-handled knife, fruit-bearing tree), tools (burning coals in pot, nine needles), and protective/ritual spaces (circle). The rituals aim to compel love or constrain will through symbolic suffering and divine invocations.
key_passages: "Thou, Belial, and thou Astaroth, and thou, Paymon, be my helpers in this undertaking."
"I baptize thee, N., in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit. Amen."

"Just as this needle is fixed in the heart of this image, so may the love of N. be fixed to the love of N., so that she cannot sleep, wake, lie down, sleep, [or] walk until she burns with love of me."
"Asyel, Castiel, Lamisniel, Rabam, Erlain, Belam, I conjure you by the true God... and in such a way that he [or she] may not be able to rest until completing my will. In the name which is Bel, etc."
"this experiment was taken [from the book] On the Secret Arts of the Imaginary Art, [from the book] On the Flowers of All Experiments, etc."

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk discusses medieval scrying-based divination, especially using children as mediums who gaze into polished surfaces (nails, mirrors, swords) to reveal hidden knowledge. It includes examples from judicial records and manuscripts such as the Rawlinson necromantic manuscript, referencing demon Berith and angelic invocations with the formula 'AGLA'. The text emphasizes the role of demons in providing visions and the theological concern over false revelations.
key_passages: within that circle they were to invoke the demon Berith, who would answer their inquiries.
writes on it the letters ‘AGLA’... 'Lord Jesus Christ, king of glory, send us three angels to tell us the truth and not falsehood in all matters about which we shall inquire.'

The general term for such divination is ‘scrying’, which is sometimes subdivided into specific forms such as catoptromancy (divination by means of a mirror), crystallomancy (by a crystal), cyclicomancy or lecanomancy (by a cup or basin filled with liquid)...
he threatened to go to a certain necromantic wizard [maleficum quendam nigromanticum] and find out through him about this strange affair.
the demon who was providing information through the boy could no longer do so once the thief had been absolved.

chunk_summaries.summary: This text describes various divinatory experiments from the Munich manuscript (Clm 849), focusing on scrying techniques using mirrors, crystals, and fingernails. It details specific rituals like the Mirror of Floron and a complex fingernail-based ritual used to detect theft or find information.
key_passages: The master... should sit and have this mirror held with both hands by a virgin boy before his chest.
The First Mirror of Lilith (spelled Lilit or Lylet) is dedicated to this ancient Hebrew demon and to her followers and her ‘knights’
the medium, usually a young boy, is vital; it is he alone who actually sees the spirits that are conjured.
take a virgin boy of legitimate birth... scrape well the thumb nail of his left hand with a knife.
The master... should look in the mirror, and he will see an armed knight seated on a horse, and his squire accompanying him.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details various divinatory rituals involving child mediums using fingernails, vessels, bones, and sleep to reveal information or find treasures. It describes specific ingredients like olive oil and bird blood, as well as the conjuration of spirits such as Apolin, Maraloth, and Berith.
key_passages: The magician commands the demons Apolin, Maraloth and Berith to send yet another demon as a teacher
the master conjures certain demon princes to come in the form of black men and appear in the boy’s nail.
Haram, a benign spirit, may come to me in my sleep and enkindle my heart and my mind, that I may know how to find a treasure
The master conjures the demons (again as often as needed) to come and appear to the boy in the forms of the thieves.
the master anoints the right shoulder blade of a ram with olive oil and puts it beneath the handle of a knife.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details a necromantic ritual from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849) for acquiring knowledge of all sciences through a demonic master spirit conjured by three kings (Apolin, Maraloth, Berith). It outlines preparation involving sleep, a protective circle, bird blood inscriptions, and a symbolic sword sacrifice. The text compares this practice to the ars notoria and medieval exempla like Caesarius of Heisterbach’s schoolboy story.
key_passages: ‘Apolin, Maraloth, Berith, I, so-and-so, exorcise and conjure you … to send me a certain spirit who is expert in teaching of all the sciences…'
When this has been said, the kings will depart and the master alone will remain, and will say to you, ‘Arise, behold your master.’
The circle required for this first experiment is a single band inscribed within a square. Inscribed in and around the band are the names of seven spirits and the four cardinal directions.
One might suppose that the magician conceives the sword further as a symbol for the power brought by his newly gained knowledge…

...the ars notoria did not call upon demonic aid, and ostensibly revolved around the recitation of devotions to the Virgin and to unfallen angels, its techniques were untraditional and superstitious...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk analyzes conjuration formulas in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on their structure (declaration, address, invocations, instruction) and content. It includes examples of named spirits (e.g., Rator et al., Vsyr et al., Lylet), sacred names used to bind demons (Aa, Ely, etc.), ritual tools (circle, ring), and a story exemplifying spirit-summoning involving Master Peter of Ardene and a Spanish magician. It also notes linguistic features (e.g., pseudo-Chaldean/Biblical/Arabic hybrid phrases) and comparative material from Jewish magic.
key_passages: The conjurations are so centrally important to necromantic experiments that the art of necromancy… can even be referred to simply as the conjuring of spirits.
Reduced to its generic essentials, the typical conjuration is in the form, ‘I conjure you, thus-and-such spirits, by the holy names, by all the saints of God, etc., to carry out my will.’
by these names, which cause you fear and terror: Rator, Lampoy, Despan, Brulo, Dronoth, Maloqui, Satola, Gelbid, Mascifin, Nartim and Lodoni

O Vsyr, Salaul, Silitor, Demor, Zanno, Syrtroy, Risbel, Cutroy, Lytay, Onor, Moloy, Pumotor, Tami, Oor and Ym, arms-bearing spirits, whose role it is to bear arms and to deceive human senses wherever you wish
by these most precious and ineffable names of the Creator of all… Aa, Ely, Sother, Adonay, Cel, Sabaoth, Messyas, Alazabra and Osian

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the structure and content of conjurations in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on the nine categories of invocations used to compel spirits—especially divine names, Christological references, and demonic hierarchies. It lists numerous divine and demonic names (e.g., Tetragrammaton, Adonay, Astaroth, Toth, Lucifer) and highlights ritual analogies drawn from biblical narratives (e.g., Jordan standing still, Red Sea parting). Special attention is given to the 'Bond of Satan or Mirage', a conjuration directed at the demon Mirage/Satan in multiple manuscript versions.
key_passages: by Toth, your prince; by Zambrim et Mambrim; by Vsuel [and] by Saduel, to whom you are held to obey and tell the truth
by the power and dignity of Lucifer, Aphaleon and Neutrion
by your master Astaroth
spirits are conjured by ‘all the experiments of Virgil [the necromancer]’
just as God commanded the Jordan and it stood still that the children of Israel might walk across without hindrance, so to I command you to obey my precepts...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk analyzes the structural and terminological parallels between official Catholic exorcism rites and necromantic conjurations, especially from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849). It highlights shared elements such as divine invocations, threats of punishment, and commands addressed to spirits. Key examples include the conjuration of Mirage, a demonic spirit who brings treasure and knowledge, and comparisons with later printed exorcism manuals. The text also discusses how necromancers borrowed clerical authority structures while adapting them for personal command over spirits.
key_passages: The conjuration of Mirage... is followed by material that the main block does not take over: a set of instructions for constructing a circle in a secret location, with various prayers, and supplementary conjurations in which Mirage is threatened with intensified pains if he does not present himself: 'If not, I exorcize you that the chains of your punishment may be rife with sulpur and pitch.'

You, therefore, most evil spirit, enemy of humankind, death’s plunderer [raptor], evader of justice [iustitie declinator], root of evils, font of vices, seducer of humans [deductor hominum] … master of demons … You, therefore, most evil Mirage, enemy of the faith of humankind, inventor [repertor] of death, herald of injustice [iniusticie declarator], root of evils, inciter of vices, seducer of humans [seductor hominis], master of demons …

The essential elements of this formula are exactly those seen in necromantic conjurations of a much later era... the exorcist insults the spirit with whom he is engaged in struggle, identifies his own action as that of Christ, and calls upon the angels and saints not simply as possessors of power that can be tapped through invocation but as active participants in the contest.

In comparison, the exorcisms cited above share their basic form (from declaration through instruction) with the necromantic conjurations we have examined, and they all rest on the mainstream theological assumption that the will of the demons can be constrained, with God’s permission and aid, and through the power of the sacred.

The Munich handbook was thus riding on the crest of a new development: what it represents is a particular version (or, from an orthodox perspective, perversion) of a more general interest in the elaboration and assembly of materials for the commanding of malign spirits.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk discusses the spirit taxonomy and naming practices in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), distinguishing between fallen demons, unfallen angels, and intermediate spirits. It lists 189 named spirits, only ~17% of which are well-attested elsewhere. The text explores theological tensions between orthodox demonology (fallen angels) and alternative daimonological concepts (neutral or benevolent spirits), referencing figures like Orient, Belial, Bartha, Lytim, and various demons with variant/corrupt names. It also notes condemnations of apocryphal angel invocations.
key_passages: Altogether 189 spirits are addressed by name in the Munich handbook’s conjurations, of whom 88 are referred to expressly as demones.
Bartha is a king; Baltim, Galtim, and Saltim are dukes; Belial is ‘foremost prince’; seven demons are ‘subjects’ of Lytim.

In two experiments the spirit addressed is named Orient… ‘O benign Orient, maker of the greater part of the world, heaven, and earth, at whose command all things on heaven and on earth were wisely made’.
Of the eleven spirits named in experiment no. 34, only Curson has broad currency (unless Hanni is equated with Haniel).
‘these spirits are between good and evil, dwelling neither in Hell nor in Paradise.’

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the demonology of the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), especially its list of eleven named spirits with ranks, appearances, and functions. It compares these to similar lists in Le livre des esperitz and later grimoires like Pseudomonarchia daemonum and Lemegeton, tracing roots to Testament of Solomon. The text also discusses medieval theological debates over demonic coercion, corporeality, and appearance—especially the insistence that demons cannot be compelled but come fraudulently—and references key figures like Johann von Frankfurt and institutions like the Paris theologians (1398).
key_passages: The most sustained discussion of demons in the Munich manual is an extended list which names and describes eleven spirits (no. 34). Each has a rank: marquis, ‘president’ (preses), count, duke, prince or king.
They may be able to take on any appearance they wish, conceivably even that of a famed beauty such as Helen of Troy... but it seems to have been agreed that their most natural shape is terrifying.
Johann von Frankfurt noted in the early fifteenth century... He insists that demons cannot be coerced: they are free agents, with intellect and will, and cannot be bound by human actions...
The service Bezlebuth performs, which would have endeared him to Faustus, is to reveal all the secrets of hell.
Identical in form but independent in content is the list contained in Le livre des esperitz found in a fifteenth-century French manuscript (Trinity College, Cambridge, MS 0.8.29).

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the structure, inscriptions, and functions of magic circles in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), listing numerous spirit names, sacred formulas, ritual positions, and symbolic elements. It compares these to broader necromantic traditions, highlights connections with astral magic (e.g., via Picatrix), and references key figures like Roger Bacon and Roger Bolingbroke. The circle is shown both as a protective boundary and as a focal point of power for spirit conjuration.
key_passages: The circles in Clm 849 consist most fundamentally of single circular bands or varying numbers of concentric bands... no. 1 has the names of the spirits Belial, Satan, Osendior, Matalot, Belzebub, Lucifer, Sententino and Apolin inscribed in and around its band
The Tetragrammaton must be inscribed on all circles, because without it 'all conjurations of the spirits will fail, and no spirit will answer truth, but falsehood'
Hunc circulum facio in nomine dei Patris omnipotentis, qui solo verbo vniuersa creauit... Hunc circulum facio in nomine Spiritus Sancti paracliti...
Tu Belial, tu Astaroth, tu Paymon, ad hoc sitis opus michi adiutores
The circle is a focus of power meant to gain control over spirits. The circles have power to convoke the demons (no. 9) and to terrify them (no. 7).

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details various magical experiments from a medieval manual, focusing on astrological timing, fumigation techniques using ingredients like myrrh and saffron, and specific rituals for taming beasts or creating concord between humans. It also describes the structure of astral magic compendiums containing lists of angels, spirits, and planetary associations.
key_passages: At this hour cast an image of the animal of the sort you wish to control or tame, and on the head of the image carve the name of the animal
make an image of yellow bronze or of yellow wax on a Thursday, and tint it with green
the work should be done under a waxing or waning moon, or in a particular phase of the moon.
In psychological experiments it is the image or the object sympathetically representing the victim that is fumigated
the boundary between astral magic and necromancy was fluid in practice even if in theory it could be stated with clarity

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk presents detailed analysis and transcription of ritual No. 1 from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), a 15th-century necromantic manual, focusing on how to conjure a master spirit for acquiring liberal arts knowledge through dream incubation. It lists multiple spirit names—both infernal (Apolin, Maraloth, Berith, Sathan, Belzebuc, etc.) and divine/angelic (Dobel, Vriel, Sabaoth, Tetragramaton, Adonay, Perarabiel, etc.)—and describes the ritual circle, blood-written inscriptions, protective cloth, sword, and writing tablet used. The text reveals a hybridized conjuration technique blending liturgical invocations with coercive demon summoning.
key_passages: Apolin, Maraloth, Berith, ego, talis, vos exorcisoa et coniuro ex parte dei omnipotentis, qui vos vestra eleccione iussit antra subire profundi...

...per hec sancta nomina quorum virtute ligamini – scilicet Dobel, Vriel, Sabaoth, Semoni, Adonay, Tetragramaton, Albumay, Siloth, Moreth, Sadabin, Rodobel, Domiel, Perarabiel,c Alatuel, nominem, nominam,d [et] Vsobel...

O Apolin, Maraloch, Berith, Sathan, Beliath, Belzebuc, [et] Lucifer, supplico vobis vt precipiatis magistro – in eo [loco] (nominando eius nomen) – vt ipse debeat venire cras ante solis ortum ad me...
...scribi debet per te cum predicto sangwine... scribe in dicto circulo nomen eius scriptum per te...
In sompno apparent tibi tres maximi reges cum famulis innumeris, militibus et peditibus, inter quos est eciam quidam magister apparens...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details four distinct magical operations: causing loss of senses via Mirael, arousing a woman's love using animal parts and spirits, gaining dignity through carved stones and silver, and inciting enmity between friends using inscribed stones and sulfur.
key_passages: Subintret Mirael cerebrum tuum et omnem sapienciam, sensum, discrecionem, [et] cogitationem diluat et aboleat.
Sicut tu, Sathan, et tu, Reuces, estis scripti in hac ymagine facta nomine talis, ita continuo affligatis brachia ista sua vt aliquid facere nequeat sed me amplecti desideret.
Ego, talis, volens talem graciam obtinere et ab eo venerari, semper honorari, similiter et timeri
Coniuro vos spiritus inimicissimos, per eterni dei gloriam, quod inter talem et talem... quantum odium inter vos est, tantum inter ipsos seminetis et inseratis.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains detailed excerpts from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including four major necromantic rituals: a nautical spirit-transport rite, horse conjuration (No. 9), resurrection of the dead (No. 10), invisibility (No. 11), and love magic (No. 12). It lists specific spirit names—Fyron, Dyspil, Onoroy, Sysabel, Cotroy, Tyroy, Rimel, Orooth; Lautrayth/Feremin/Oliroomim; Brimer/Suburith/Tranauit/Lyroth/Beryen/Damayn; Fyriel/Mememil/Berith/Taraor; and Belial/Astaroth/Paymon—and describes their invocation via protective circles, ritual tools (sword, bridle, golden ring), and ingredients (blessed water, incense, virgin wax). The rituals blend Christian invocations with demonic evocation, reflecting the hybrid theological logic of medieval magical practice.
key_passages: hoc circulo, ego invoco vos per vestram subieccionem michi factam, vt statim sine mora subito et incontinenti hincp debeatis accedere...

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk contains extensive liturgical and consecratory material from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focused on ritual book and experiment consecration. The texts emphasize the authority of the exorcist over demonic forces (aereas potestates, infernales principes) through sacred names and biblical allusions. Ritual steps include nine-day preparation, Confiteor recitation, aspersion with holy water, and sign of the cross. Several esoteric name-formulas appear (e.g., Stimlamathon, Otaizomos), likely reflecting transmission from Arabic or Byzantine grimoire traditions.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details the Munich Handbook’s (CLM 849) necromantic rituals, focusing on weekday-based conjurations of angels and spirits. It lists angelic rulers for each day (Raphael/Sol to Captiel/Saturnus), their attendant spirits, planetary correspondences, seals, suffumigations (e.g., assandalum croceum for Sunday, aloes for Monday), and hour-specific divine names and functions. The text includes detailed invocations using layered divine names (Adonay, Sadaye, Cados, Yma, etc.), instructions for image-making (e.g., silver dog for tongue-binding), and references to protective circles inscribed with Trinitarian formulas and angelic names.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk details conjurations and ritual procedures from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on image magic for Friday (Venus) and Saturday (Saturn). It includes lists of angels, winds, planetary rulers, and temporal hierarchies tied to months, days, hours, and cardinal directions. The Saturn section describes binding rituals involving images made of pitch, with specific incantations invoking spirits like Castuel, Matraton, Satael, and Amamnim. Temporal frameworks include 12 monthly princes (e.g., Ragiel, Dyrnaot), four seasonal divisions each governed by three angels, and daily/hourly angelic rulers (e.g., Raphael for Sunday/first hour). Protective circles, ritual tools (gladius), and ingredients (cera alba, pice clara) are also specified.

The rituals require protective circles, ritual tools (sword, knife, tripod stool), olive oil, and strict procedural steps. Named spirits include Astaroth, Belzebub, Berit, and multiple demonic entities with Greek-sounding names, alongside divine names like Tetragramaton and Alpha et O.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk presents a theological-historical argument linking ancient pagan religious systems—including Egyptian, Babylonian, and other Near Eastern traditions—to a corrupted form of primal monotheism. It emphasizes the worship of the dead as a stepping-stone to spirit worship (daimonia), describes ritual practices such as the Greater Mysteries, human sacrifice, and offerings for the dead (including Purgatory), and identifies Osiris/Asar, Semiramis, Zerbanit, and the Sun god (often identified with the Serpent) as central deities. The text frames these traditions as part of a deliberate post-Flood restoration of idolatry that perverts original patriarchal faith.
key_passages: "The worship of the dead a stepping-stone to worship of daimonia—Influence of priesthood through their magical powers"
"Idols the habitation of the daimonia — Testimony of Spiritualists and Buddhists — Haunted houses, etc."
"Human sacrifices to the Pagan god by the Cross or by fire, both being symbols or manifestations of the god"
"The Round Cake, the symbol of the Sun god, offered for the sins of the people Purgatory — Sacrifices for the dead — Peuance"
"Initiates prepared by fasting and confession—Description of Greater Mysteries—Their object the revelation of the god—The dread secret or "Apporeta"—Revelation of the god as the Serpent"

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk explores cross-cultural parallels in death rituals and ancestor veneration, emphasizing the near-universal timing of such festivals around November or early spring, often linked to Pleiades visibility. It traces shared motifs—including mourning for Osiris/Adonis, Roman Feralia/Lemuria, Greek Anthesteria—to a common primordial origin, which the text attributes to Babylon and the Deluge tradition. The chapter argues that ancient deities originated as deified ancestors whose worship became entangled with astral phenomena and cosmological cycles.
key_passages: "Wherever the Roman Catholic Church exists, solemn Mass for All Souls is said on the 2nd November..."
"The Egyptians began their year at the same time as the Jews, and on the seventeenth day of their second month commenced their solemn mourning for Osiris, the Lord of Tombs..."
"In Rome the festival of the dead, or 'Feralia,' called 'Dii Manes,' or 'the day of the spirits of the dead'...
"The festival of the dead was held on the 28th November [MS: likely typo for 1st Nov], and was regulated by the Pleiades..."
"Hesiod...says that 'the gods were the souls of men who were afterwards worshipped by their posterity, on account of their extraordinary virtues.'"

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk presents extensive patristic and classical evidence for the theory that pagan gods originated as deified human ancestors—particularly heroes, kings, and patriarchs like Nimrod, Ham, Osiris, Cronus, and Belus—who were worshipped after death. It traces how ancestor veneration evolved into idolatry and astral theology, with spirits believed to inhabit celestial bodies. The text explicitly links these beliefs to Babylonian origins and connects them to the broader Mediterranean religious tradition.
key_passages: "the gods were the souls of men who were afterwards worshipped by their posterity, on account of their extraordinary virtues." (Hesiod)
"Osiris...was a holy man, whose soul was worshipped after his death by the Egyptians." (pseudo-Hermes Trismegistus)
"Cronus, Osiris, Horus, and all their other principal deities were once here men, but that after they died their souls migrated into some one or other of the heavenly bodies..." (Plutarch)
"Belus was the first king of Babylon...after his death was worshipped as a god." (Castor, quoted)
"The gods first worshipped are the same persons, men and women, even to his time received and worshipped as gods." (Eusebius)

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk traces comparative mythology across Egypt, India, and the Near East, arguing for a Cushite origin of phallic/Sun worship and linking Osiris, Siva, Nimrod, and other deities as overlapping figures. It emphasizes ancestor veneration, funerary rites (e.g., suttee), and the identification of Indian Yama with Osiris, as well as early Buddhist cosmology involving multiple Buddhas.
key_passages: But the worship of ancestors seems to have been a part of the idolatry propagated by Nimrod, for we are told that Osiris built a temple in Egypt to his grandfather Ham...
Siva...is represented as making a voyage during the Deluge on the ship Argha?
The Indian 'Yama' seems to be another form of Osiris. Like the latter, he is the judge of the dead, and weighs their good actions against their bad actions...
Cama was originally Kham or Ham...and his wife was 'Rhytia,' who are manifestly the same as Cama and Reti.
'The Suttees, who devoted themselves on the funeral pyres of their husbands, were considered to become pure by burning'

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk draws extensive comparative parallels between Buddhist death rituals, ancestor veneration, spirit communication, and magical practices with those of ancient Egypt, Chaldea, Greece, and Rome. It highlights the use of relics, consecration rites, merit-making (Kungte), planchette-based ghostly communications, and asceticism to attain supernatural powers (iddhi, abhinna). The text explicitly links Buddhist necromancy with Chaldean magic and emphasizes the role of spirits—especially deceased saints—in mediating magical efficacy.
key_passages: The Buddhist temple...is the home of marvels; and in front of many statues of Buddha there is a table in China on which an apparatus similar to a planchette is used for ghostly communications. This planchette has been known for many hundred years.
'It was plainly,' says Mr Lillie, 'an elaborate apparatus to nullify the action of evil spirits by the aid of good spirits.'

A saint dies, and is buried in a tumulus, or under a tree, and under this tree, by-and-by, sits another holy man who periodically gets obsessed by the dead saint, and in that state exhibits the various marvels of clairvoyance, fortune-telling, etc.
'Shu tsui,' 'redeem from guilt'—this is expressed as the phrase used for the priests’ power to save souls post-mortem.

The name given to these ascetics was 'Shramanas,' or 'Shamanas,' a word meaning 'quieting of the passions,' the object of asceticism being the complete subjugation of every natural desire as a means to the attainment of these supernatural powers.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk examines comparative ancient religious practices across Celtic, Mexican, Peruvian and Egyptian cultures, emphasizing their shared roots in Babylonian/Chaldean traditions. It identifies Cush as the human origin of Hermes/Buddha/Woden and links Druidic fire rituals (Beltane), human sacrifice, and cross-groves to Mesopotamian origins. The text connects necromantic/magical practices across cultures through Accadian magic, Shamanism, and Hermetic traditions.

"the most ancient or mythological Buddha with Cush, i.e., Hermes... the votaries of Theosophy and Spiritualism... speak of [Buddhism] as 'the teaching of Hermes'

"it was identical with the Shamanism of the Ural-Altaic races, and with that of the Tartars and Mongols of Eastern Asia—that is to say, with the magic and necromancy practised by the Shamanas or priests of Buddhism"

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk explores the intersection of ancient wisdom traditions (Buddhism, Hermeticism), spirit invocation practices, and early Christian critiques of pagan necromancy. It argues that the 'powers' claimed by Buddha and Hermes—retrospection, desire-fulfillment, incantatory efficacy—rely on interaction with spirits (daimonia), identified in Christian theology as demons or fallen angels. Rituals such as temple sleep, oracle consultation, and spirit possession are analyzed as mechanisms for accessing supernatural agency, especially in healing and divination contexts.
key_passages: Augustine quotes Hermes Trismegistus as stating 'that visible and tangible images... are... the bodies of the gods, and that there dwell in them certain spirits which have been invited to come in them, and which have power to inflict harm, or to fulfil the desires of those by whom divine honours and services are rendered to them.'

It is clear also that the spirits primarily invoked by Hermes or Buddha... were not those of the persons afterwards worshipped as gods... If not, they must have been simply the same daimonia as those mentioned in the New Testament, namely, spirits of evil who produced in those they possessed various forms of disease or insanity

The human originals of the Pagan gods were, at the most, three individuals... but what the people generally worshipped were certain beings clothed with certain characteristics, powers and attributes, whose spirits were supposed to inhabit certain images, shrines, temples or other places, and these... were devils, i.e., 'daimonia,' or evil spirits

These spirits lurk under the statues and consecrated images. These inspire the breasts of their prophets with their afflatus, animate the fibres of the entrails, direct the flight of birds, rule the lots, give efficiency to oracles...

The same symptoms occurred in the case of the Pythoness of the Delphic Oracle, and as it is clear, from the notice in the Acts, that this was due to possession by a demon, we may conclude that the Theomanteis were similarly possessed.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk argues that spirit communication practices—including ancient necromancy, pagan theurgy, Christian saint veneration, and modern Spiritualism—are all manifestations of the same underlying phenomenon: communication with evil spirits (daimonia) rather than departed souls. It traces historical continuity from Hermes Trismegistus through late antique Theurgists to medieval Catholic saints and 19th-century Spiritualism, identifying consistent patterns of levitation, materialization, clairvoyance, and spirit manifestation as evidence of demonic agency.

the spirits invoked are supposed to be, and claim themselves to be, the spirits of departed relatives or friends... This was the character of the magic taught by Hermes Trismegistus.
My position, therefore, is that the phenomena of Spiritualism in their entirety do not require further confirmation. They are proved quite as well as any facts are proved in other sciences.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk explores ancient and medieval spirit communication practices, emphasizing the continuity between Pagan necromancy (especially via mesmerism), early Christian critiques (e.g., Tertullian), and later esoteric traditions. It argues that trance states—achieved through gazing, fasting, or self-induction—facilitated possession by daimonia (spirits/demons), with mediums (including boys, virgins, Fakirs) acting as vessels for prophecy, healing, or divination. The text explicitly links mesmerism, electrobiology, and hypnotism to ancient magical rituals and identifies them as modern manifestations of the same spirit-possession framework.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk argues that modern spiritualist and theosophical spirit communication is a revival of ancient pagan necromancy, rooted in pre-diluvian traditions taught by figures like Hermes/Cush and Chrysor/Hephaestus. It draws parallels between ancient methods (e.g., table-turning with planchette) and modern mediumship, asserting that all such spirits are daimonia—evil spirits masquerading as ancestors or gods.

chunk_summaries.summary: This chunk explores the antediluvian and postdiluvian origins of spirit communication, linking biblical Nephilim (Fallen Ones) to pagan demon worship. It argues that Cush/Nimrod revived pre-Flood magical traditions involving spirit invocation, and identifies Oannes/Dagon and Chrysor as Nephilim or demonic beings who taught humans necromantic arts. The text connects Noahic tradition with later mythologies, suggesting pagan deities were deified postdiluvian spirits or demon-human hybrids.
key_passages: it is very probable that Cush, the originator of postdiluvian idolatry, may... have received instructions from the spirits he invoked as gods, as to the means of communicating with them and enlisting their powers on his behalf; or, in other words, the principles of magic and sorcery
